patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 62-year-old man, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I keep getting these bad headaches and my vision gets weird sometimes.” Volunteer: bad headaches most days for months (sometimes wake you), blurry/double vision when severe, and feeling tired/run down even after sleep. If asked about other symptoms, mention: shaky hands/sweats when you haven’t eaten, sudden hunger relieved by juice/sugar; rings tighter/shoes don’t fit; face looks puffier; lower sex drive; higher blood pressure recently. Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: louder snoring, witnessed breathing pauses, unrefreshed sleep; bite/jaw feels misaligned; joint aches in hands/knees; oilier skin/acne. Be reluctant/embarrassed and only admit if asked directly: erectile problems (or any nipple discharge). Mention misleading episodes only if asked about allergies: a couple times lips looked swollen and once throat felt tight after new food—worry it could be angioedema. Express money worries: assume expensive = best but fear bills/insurance won’t cover imaging/new treatments; prefer postponing visits to keep part-time work. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer fully, and ask about costs and time off.

medications: metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
amlodipine 10 mg PO daily
lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly
omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
confounders: Poorly controlled diabetes can elevate IGF-1 variability and complicate symptom attribution (fatigue, neuropathy)
Obesity and suspected obstructive sleep apnea can contribute to fatigue, headaches, and hypertension independent of GH excess
Mild hyperprolactinemia may suggest pituitary adenoma and distract from ectopic GHRH source
GERD treated with PPI may mask upper GI symptoms from a neuroendocrine tumor source

acceptable_alternatives: Acromegaly due to ectopic GHRH secretion
required_evidence: Elevated age-adjusted serum IGF-1
Failure of GH suppression on 75 g OGTT (GH nadir not suppressed)
Elevated plasma GHRH supporting ectopic GHRH secretion
Exclude primary hypothyroidism and hypercortisolism as confounders (normal TSH/free T4; appropriate AM cortisol if clinically indicated)

expected_lab_results.Serum IGF-1: Elevated above age- and sex-adjusted reference range
expected_lab_results.Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) - GH: Fails to suppress; GH nadir remains elevated (not suppressed below assay-specific cutoff, typically >1 ng/mL; >0.4 ng/mL with ultrasensitive assays)
expected_lab_results.Plasma GHRH: Elevated (supports ectopic GHRH source rather than pituitary somatotroph adenoma alone)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal or mildly elevated (possible pituitary hyperplasia/stalk effect)
expected_lab_results.AM cortisol (08:00): Normal (not suggestive of adrenal insufficiency; if low would prompt confirmatory testing before any major intervention)

medications: Octreotide LAR 20 mg IM every 4 weeks (titrate to 30 mg IM every 4 weeks based on IGF-1/GH response and tolerance) OR Lanreotide depot 90 mg deep SC every 4 weeks (titrate to 120 mg every 4 weeks)
Pegvisomant 10 mg SC daily (titrate by IGF-1 to 20–30 mg SC daily) if IGF-1 remains elevated despite somatostatin analog or if not tolerated
Cabergoline 0.5 mg PO twice weekly (titrate up to 1 mg PO twice weekly) as adjunct if mild biochemical activity and/or prolactin co-secretion
non_pharmacological: Localize and treat the ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor (cross-sectional imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis and functional imaging such as somatostatin receptor imaging; refer to endocrine oncology/surgery for definitive resection when feasible)
Baseline and periodic monitoring: IGF-1 every 3–6 months until controlled; GH as supportive; assess glucose tolerance/HbA1c, blood pressure, sleep apnea, and cardiomyopathy as part of acromegaly care

If tumor unresectable or metastatic: consider long-term somatostatin analog therapy and multidisciplinary oncologic management (including peptide receptor radionuclide therapy when appropriate for somatostatin receptor–positive disease)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck IGF-1 (and clinical symptoms) 12 weeks after initiating or adjusting long-acting somatostatin analog therapy; earlier (4–6 weeks) after starting/titrating pegvisomant; ongoing follow-up every 3–6 months once controlled

must_include: Labs suggest acromegaly physiology (high IGF-1 and GH not suppressed by glucose)
Elevated GHRH points to an ectopic hormone-secreting tumor (GRFoma) rather than a typical pituitary adenoma
Next step is to localize the source with imaging and coordinate specialist care
Definitive treatment is removal or control of the tumor when possible
Medical therapy can control hormone excess (somatostatin analogs; pegvisomant; sometimes cabergoline)
Monitoring plan using IGF-1 to guide dose adjustments and assess response
Discuss expected benefits (symptom improvement, reduced long-term complications) and realistic timelines

Review key adverse effects: somatostatin analogs (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes), pegvisomant (liver enzyme elevation), cabergoline (nausea, orthostasis; rare valvulopathy with higher cumulative doses)
When to seek urgent care (severe abdominal pain, jaundice, signs of hyperglycemia/hypoglycemia, allergic reactions)
Cardiometabolic risk reduction: blood pressure, diabetes screening/management, healthy diet and activity
Sleep apnea screening if symptoms (snoring, daytime somnolence)
Importance of adherence to follow-up labs/imaging and multidisciplinary care

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for acromegaly physiology and ectopic GHRH tumor clues; assess comorbidities (HTN/DM/OSA), medication list, pregnancy status, and red flags.

Order/confirm diagnostic and baseline labs: IGF-1, GH with oral glucose suppression test (if not already done), GHRH level, fasting glucose/HbA1c, CMP/LFTs, lipids; consider prolactin if considering cabergoline; baseline thyroid function as clinically indicated.
Review labs to support diagnosis: elevated IGF-1, GH not suppressed by glucose, elevated GHRH; review baseline metabolic and liver parameters for therapy selection.
Diagnose acromegaly physiology due to ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) based on elevated GHRH plus acromegaly labs; note need for localization imaging and specialist coordination.

Explain diagnosis and next steps (localization imaging and multidisciplinary care), explain treatment options (tumor control/removal; medical therapy), monitoring plan (IGF-1-guided adjustments), adverse effects, and urgent return precautions; provide lifestyle/cardiometabolic and OSA screening education.
Critical safety check before prescribing: verify allergies to somatostatin analogs (e.g., octreotide/lanreotide), pegvisomant, cabergoline, excipients/latex as applicable.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions (e.g., cabergoline with dopamine antagonists; additive hypotension with antihypertensives; glucose-lowering therapy adjustments with somatostatin analogs).

Initiate appropriate medical therapy if indicated while awaiting localization/definitive management (typically long-acting somatostatin analog; consider pegvisomant if inadequate control or contraindications; consider cabergoline in selected cases), with lab monitoring plan.

Arrange follow-up for IGF-1 reassessment and dose titration (e.g., 6–12 weeks after therapy initiation/adjustment), LFT monitoring if pegvisomant, glucose monitoring, and coordination with endocrinology/oncology/surgery and imaging.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male warehouse supervisor. You are cooperative and speak clearly, but you look visibly short of breath and speak in short sentences. Start by saying: “I can’t catch my breath, and it’s gotten really bad over the last few days.” Early in the visit, volunteer: short of breath even at rest/talking, dry cough, wiped out/weak, low fever with chills, fast shallow breathing. If asked for details, add: began like a bad cold/flu then suddenly worsened; chest tightness with deep breaths; winded walking to the bathroom; occasional bluish lips/fingertips when struggling; waking up gasping at night. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: racing heart during episodes, reduced urination from poor intake, poor appetite, lightheadedness on standing, feeling foggy/confused at worst. Express a misconception: worry it’s your heart because of chest pressure; mention mild ankle swelling by day’s end if asked about swelling. Ask about costs: nintedanib affordability/cheaper alternatives, missing overtime and returning to full duties quickly, insurance for home oxygen, imaging, and pulmonary rehab. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it briefly and continue answering questions.

medications: Nintedanib 150 mg PO twice daily
Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Albuterol inhaler 90 mcg 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed
comorbidities: Former smoker (25 pack-years, quit 5 years ago)
confounders: Recent upper respiratory viral-like illness (fever, myalgias) raising concern for viral pneumonia
Former smoker with intermittent wheeze suggesting COPD/asthma overlap symptoms
Mild transaminitis likely related to nintedanib complicating medication choices and interpretation of systemic illness
Elevated D-dimer in acute inflammation can mimic pulmonary embolism risk and prompt alternative workup

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP; idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage/ARDS pattern)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) of unclear trigger (if idiopathic status not established yet)
Acute exacerbation of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (if prior IPF history is later identified)
required_evidence: Acute/subacute onset (days to weeks) of severe dyspnea and hypoxemia
Arterial blood gas on room air showing marked hypoxemia (low PaO2 and SaO2) often with respiratory alkalosis early
No evidence for cardiogenic pulmonary edema/heart failure as primary cause (clinical assessment ± echocardiography if available)
Clinical exclusion of alternative causes such as infection, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndrome (history, exam, and targeted testing as indicated)
Imaging consistent with diffuse bilateral interstitial/alveolar opacities (if available) and overall presentation consistent with diffuse alveolar damage

expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - pH: Often mildly elevated early (respiratory alkalosis), e.g., ~7.45–7.50; may normalize/acidify if fatigue progresses
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - PaCO2: Low early due to tachypnea, e.g., ~28–34 mmHg; may rise if ventilatory failure develops
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - PaO2: Markedly decreased (severe hypoxemia), commonly <60 mmHg on room air
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - HCO3-: Normal to mildly decreased (acute compensation), e.g., ~20–24 mEq/L
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - SaO2: Reduced, often <90% on room air (may be significantly lower depending on severity)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: May be normal or mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis); absence of marked leukocytosis supports noninfectious etiology but does not exclude infection
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often normal; thrombocytopenia would raise concern for sepsis/DIC or other systemic process

medications: Oxygen therapy titrated to maintain SpO2 92-96% (or PaO2 60-80 mmHg) via nasal cannula/nonrebreather; escalate as needed
Methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day IV (or equivalent systemic corticosteroid) with reassessment over 48-72 hours
Nintedanib: hold during acute critical illness; reassess for restart after stabilization and after excluding contraindications (e.g., active bleeding, severe hepatic dysfunction)
non_pharmacological: Admit to hospital; ICU-level monitoring if severe hypoxemia or escalating oxygen needs

Escalate respiratory support as needed: high-flow nasal cannula; consider noninvasive ventilation only in carefully selected patients; early intubation if worsening work of breathing or refractory hypoxemia
Lung-protective ventilation strategy if intubated (low tidal volume ~6 mL/kg predicted body weight; plateau pressure <30 cm H2O) and appropriate PEEP
Evaluate and treat reversible triggers while maintaining AIP as primary working diagnosis: infection workup and empiric antibiotics only if clinical suspicion for infection is present
DVT prophylaxis (mechanical and/or pharmacologic per bleeding risk) and early mobilization as tolerated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after initiating oxygen/support; daily inpatient reassessment; post-discharge pulmonology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (earlier if oxygen-dependent)

must_include: AIP is a sudden, severe inflammation/injury of the lung tissue causing low oxygen levels
Diagnosis is based on clinical picture and exclusion of other causes (infection, heart failure, clots), and may require imaging and specialist input
Condition can be life-threatening and may require ICU care and ventilatory support
Immediate goal is to improve oxygenation and reduce work of breathing; escalation of respiratory support may be needed
Systemic corticosteroids are often used despite limited high-quality evidence; response will be closely monitored
Nintedanib is not an acute rescue therapy; it may be held during acute instability and reconsidered later
Discuss prognosis uncertainty and need for close monitoring
Review medication allergies (penicillin) and how this affects antibiotic choices if infection is suspected
Elicit patient goals/preferences regarding intubation and ICU-level interventions if deterioration occurs
Avoid smoking and respiratory irritants; ensure vaccinations are up to date (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19 as applicable)
Explain home oxygen safety if discharged with oxygen
Provide return precautions: worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, or low oxygen readings

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Often normal in infancy/early childhood; may be elevated if obesity/insulin resistance has developed
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Typically normal early; may be elevated if impaired glucose tolerance/diabetes has developed
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: May be normal early; can be elevated with insulin resistance (especially with weight gain)
expected_lab_results.TSH: May be normal or low/normal (central hypothyroidism possible)
expected_lab_results.Free T4: May be low or low-normal with inappropriately normal/low TSH (suggesting central hypothyroidism)
expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Often low for age (suggesting growth hormone deficiency/insufficiency)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 2-year-old girl’s primary caregiver, anxious and vague. Start with: “My baby suddenly started having weird twisting movements and can’t sit like he used to.” Volunteer only these at first: increasing stiffness in arms/legs over days, dropping toys/not using hands, more fussy especially at night. If asked about timeline/illness, mention a fever/runny nose a week ago before this started. If asked about spells, describe brief staring/eyes rolling back; avoid the word “seizure” unless directly asked, then admit you’re scared it might be seizures. If feeding/swallowing asked, disclose coughing/choking with drinks and occasional grunting/brief bluish face; express guilt. If asked about development, say less babbling/less social and lost ability to pull to stand. If asked about hydration/urine, say fewer wet diapers some days. Only if asked about triggers, reveal worse when fasting (morning), sweating/fast breathing/hot spells, and prior episodes after not eating well. Bring up misconceptions: tick hike worry, fruit juice/sugar causing it, family loose joints/bruising. Ask about cost: want minimum visits, can you “just treat symptoms,” suspicious generics, worried PT/OT won’t be covered. If clinician is empathetic, soften, share more details, and ask what is dangerous and what you should do tonight.

expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (HCO3-): Normal to mildly decreased; can be decreased with metabolic stress
expected_lab_results.Anion gap: Normal to mildly elevated (may be elevated if lactic acidosis present)
expected_lab_results.Lactate: Normal to elevated (often elevated if mitochondrial dysfunction or acute illness)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose: Usually normal (hypoglycemia would raise concern for fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal (may vary with hydration status)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Often normal (may be elevated with acidosis/dehydration)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often normal (may be increased in non-anion-gap acidosis or with saline administration)

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment priorities, address uncertainty/differential/prognosis, and provide lifestyle/sick-day guidance (avoid fasting, early care during illness, follow-up adherence).
Critical safety check before any medication orders, confirming reported amoxicillin-clavulanate rash and documenting reaction type/severity.
Noncritical safety check prior to prescribing (e.g., thiamine/biotin, anticonvulsants, antibiotics) to avoid interactions with current meds.

Initiate empiric, potentially reversible therapies and supportive orders consistent with results/clinical status: thiamine and biotin; dextrose-containing IV fluids if hypoglycemia/poor intake; seizure control if present; avoid contraindicated agents based on metabolic suspicion.

Critical check before finalizing prescriptions to ensure no contraindications (e.g., avoid valproate in suspected mitochondrial disease; avoid prolonged fasting; avoid inappropriate fluids/meds in metabolic acidosis context).
Arrange inpatient monitoring and specialty follow-up: neurology and metabolic genetics; plan for repeat labs (glucose, lactate, pH) and review MRI/EEG results.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed, key counseling delivered, and follow-up/monitoring plan documented.

required_evidence: Clinical pattern consistent with congenital myasthenic syndrome: lifelong/childhood-onset fluctuating fatigable weakness (often ocular/bulbar/limb-girdle) with possible episodic respiratory decompensation
Absence of features suggesting autoimmune myasthenia gravis (e.g., no clear later-life onset; no thymoma history; seronegativity if tested)
Electrodiagnostic evidence of neuromuscular junction transmission defect (e.g., decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
Genetic confirmation of a CMS glycosylation-pathway variant (e.g., GMPPB, DPAGT1, ALG2, ALG14) OR prior documented diagnosis consistent with this subtype
Medication history showing worsening with agents known to exacerbate NMJ disorders (e.g., macrolides such as azithromycin)

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Bicarbon: Normal at baseline; may be elevated if chronic hypoventilation/CO2 retention is present

Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 3000 mg/day unless otherwise directed)
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications that can worsen neuromuscular junction transmission (especially macrolides such as azithromycin; also avoid aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones when possible, magnesium-containing products, and neuromuscular blockers unless essential and closely monitored)
Respiratory safety plan: assess for bulbar/respiratory weakness; provide clear return precautions for dyspnea, inability to clear secretions, dysphagia/aspiration, or rapidly worsening weakness

Referral/follow-up with neuromuscular specialist/genetics for confirmation of subtype and individualized therapy (including consideration of 3,4-diaminopyridine or ephedrine in selected CMS subtypes under specialist supervision)
Energy conservation and graded activity with physical therapy as tolerated; avoid overexertion that triggers fatigability
Swallow safety counseling if bulbar symptoms: small bites, upright posture, consider speech/swallow evaluation if choking/coughing with meals
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular specialist follow-up within 2-4 weeks (earlier within 48-72 hours if recent exacerbation or respiratory/bulbar symptoms); primary care follow-up within 1-2 weeks for medication tolerability and vitals

must_include: This is a genetic (inherited) neuromuscular junction disorder causing fatigable weakness; it is not autoimmune myasthenia gravis

Goal of pyridostigmine: improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigable weakness; expected onset and common side effects (cramps, diarrhea, increased secretions)
Adjunct option (albuterol/salbutamol) rationale in some glycosylation-related CMS and key side effects (tremor, palpitations)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for anemia (fatigue, pallor, pica), infections (frequency/severity), periodic fevers (pattern/triggers), developmental history, family history/consanguinity, diet/iron exposure, prior transfusions, and current meds; explicitly confirm antibiotic allergy history.

Interpret results for hypoproliferative microcytic anemia (low MCV with inappropriately low retic), iron status (avoid assuming iron deficiency), and evidence of antibody deficiency (low immunoglobulins/poor titers).

Before any prescribing, confirm and document allergy details (urticarial rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate), severity, timing, and tolerated alternatives; ensure allergy is communicated for future infection treatment.
Optional but expected if prescribing: screen for interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., folate/B6 interactions are uncommon but check for anticonvulsants, warfarin, etc.).

Initiate supportive therapy consistent with plan (e.g., pyridoxine and folate trial; arrange IVIG/SCIG via immunology/hematology pathway rather than ad-hoc), and specify monitoring (repeat CBC/retic) and safety counseling; avoid empiric iron unless iron deficiency is demonstrated.

Arrange timely hematology/immunology follow-up, infusion scheduling if starting IVIG/SCIG, lab recheck interval, and developmental services coordination; provide a fever action plan and return precautions.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you keep getting awful one-sided headaches around your left eye/temple that hit out of nowhere. Volunteer: attacks ramp up fast, feel unbearable; left eye waters/gets red; same-side nose runny/stuffy; you feel restless and can’t sit still; this has happened almost daily for the last few weeks.

If asked, add: each attack lasts 45–60 minutes; often at night and wakes you around the same time; 1–3 attacks/day; between attacks you’re mostly normal; little light/noise sensitivity; usually no nausea; alcohol reliably triggers during these weeks; your dad had “cluster headaches.”

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: eyelid droop, facial sweating/heat, smaller pupil during attacks, avoiding sleep out of fear.

Withhold and deny if asked about meds/opioids: you are not using anyone else’s strong pain pills and don’t want it documented.

Express misconceptions: you suspect sinus infection, dental/TMJ due to jaw tightness.

If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and open up more. Voice cost worries and preference to try cheaper options first; question imaging/oxygen/injections coverage.

medications: lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
nicotine transdermal patch 21 mg/24 hr daily
confounders: Tobacco and alcohol as potential headache triggers (alcohol reliably precipitates attacks during cluster periods)
Uncontrolled hypertension contributing to headache concern and limiting some acute options if severe
Possible obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, nonrestorative sleep) that can worsen headache burden and mimic morning headaches
Family history of migraine in addition to familial cluster headache, increasing diagnostic overlap
Intermittent unilateral nasal congestion and tearing could be misattributed to allergic rhinitis or sinusitis

required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attacks occur in clusters (e.g., 1 every other day up to 8/day) with circadian/circannual pattern
At least one ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptom during attacks (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis)
Restlessness/agitation during attacks (prefers pacing vs lying still)
No focal neurologic deficits between attacks; normal neurologic exam
Family history of cluster headache supports familial form

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected in primary headache disorder)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (needed before lithium or frequent NSAID use; not required for oxygen/triptan)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes at attack onset PRN
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at attack onset PRN; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil immediate-release 80 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days based on response and ECG (typical target 240-480 mg/day)
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days (bridge therapy while verapamil takes effect)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during active cluster period (common trigger)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Keep a headache diary (timing, duration, triggers, response to therapies)
Consider referral to neurology/headache specialist for confirmation, preventive titration, and consideration of alternatives (e.g., galcanezumab, lithium, occipital nerve block) if refractory
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess response and adverse effects; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if attacks remain uncontrolled or medication side effects occur

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder (not sinus infection) characterized by short, very severe unilateral attacks with autonomic symptoms
Typical pattern: attacks come in bouts (weeks to months) with symptom-free periods; familial tendency can occur
Red flags that would require urgent evaluation (new neurologic deficits, sudden 'worst headache', fever/neck stiffness, new headache after age >50, immunosuppression, cancer history)
Acute/abortive plan: high-flow oxygen and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan at onset; do not use oral triptans as primary abortive due to slower onset
Preventive plan: verapamil is first-line; may take time to work so a short steroid taper can bridge
How to use oxygen safely (non-rebreather mask, no smoking/open flames)
Sumatriptan limits and key adverse effects (chest tightness, flushing) and when to seek urgent care (severe chest pain, neurologic symptoms)
Confirm sulfonamide antibiotic allergy and clarify reaction (urticaria) and reassure that proposed cluster therapies are not sulfonamide antibiotics
Screen for cardiovascular history and risk factors before triptan use (CAD, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension)
Discuss verapamil monitoring needs (baseline and follow-up ECG for PR prolongation/heart block; symptoms of bradycardia, dizziness, syncope)
Discuss steroid short-course risks (insomnia, mood changes, hyperglycemia, GI upset) and mitigation strategies
Avoid alcohol during cluster periods and identify personal triggers
Sleep regularity and stress management can reduce attack frequency
Importance of adherence to preventive therapy and keeping follow-up for dose titration and monitoring
When to seek emergency care: uncontrolled severe pain despite abortives, new neurologic symptoms, severe chest pain after triptan, or oxygen safety issues

Start with: “I keep getting these bad headaches and my eyesight feels off.” Early on, volunteer: dull headaches behind your eyes getting more frequent over months; worsening side vision (bumping into things/missing things); intermittent blurriness worse during headaches; increased fatigue despite sleep. If asked about associated symptoms, add: nausea without much vomiting, more light sensitivity, occasional double vision when looking to the side, decreased sex drive, irregular/lighter periods, a couple episodes of milky nipple discharge when you squeezed, increased thirst/urination including nighttime, easier weight gain and feeling colder. Do not mention unless specifically probed: brain fog/forgetfulness, dry skin/thinning hair, lightheadedness on standing, constipation, puffy hands/feet. Be reluctant/embarrassed about orgasm difficulty, avoiding sex, breast leakage, and feeling down/irritable; disclose only with gentle, nonjudgmental questions. Express misconceptions: you think it’s migraines, eye strain, sinus pressure, or caffeine. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit worry about serious causes and costs (MRIs/labs, surgery time off, long-term hormone meds).

confounders: SSRI (sertraline) associated with SIADH/hyponatremia, complicating interpretation of low sodium in suspected secondary adrenal insufficiency

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary dermoid or epidermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar inclusion cyst)
required_evidence: Sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion on MRI consistent with dermoid/epidermoid (epidermoid shows diffusion restriction; dermoid often T1 hyperintense due to fat)
Symptoms attributable to sellar mass effect (e.g., headache, visual field defect) and/or pituitary dysfunction
Exclusion of more common sellar masses (pituitary adenoma, Rathke cleft cyst, craniopharyngioma) based on imaging characteristics and clinical course
Assessment for diabetes insipidus if polyuria/polydipsia is present (serum/urine osmolality pattern consistent with central DI)

expected_lab_results.Sodium (Na): Often normal; may be elevated if central diabetes insipidus is present (hypernatremia possible, especially with limited water intake)
expected_lab_results.Chloride (Cl): Typically normal; may be mildly elevated with hypernatremia/dehydration
expected_lab_results.BUN: Normal to mildly elevated if dehydration from diabetes insipidus
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Normal; increased if diabetes insipidus with free-water loss
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Normal; in central diabetes insipidus: inappropriately low/dilute despite elevated serum osmolality

medications: Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO twice daily (only if central diabetes insipidus is present; titrate to symptoms and sodium)
non_pharmacological: Urgent neurosurgery referral for consideration of surgical resection/decompression (definitive management; goal is symptom relief and tissue diagnosis)
Endocrinology consultation for full pituitary hormone evaluation and perioperative management (not limited to basic electrolytes)
Ophthalmology/visual field (formal perimetry) assessment if any visual symptoms or suprasellar extension suspected
If diabetes insipidus suspected: monitor strict intake/output, daily weights, and serial sodium/serum osmolality during initiation/titration of desmopressin
Patient counseling on red flags requiring urgent evaluation: worsening headache, visual changes, confusion, severe thirst/polyuria, fever/meningismus (rare chemical meningitis if rupture)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurosurgery and endocrinology follow-up within 1–2 weeks (sooner/urgent if visual deficits, progressive symptoms, or significant sodium abnormalities); repeat MRI interval per neurosurgery (commonly 3–6 months post-op or if managed conservatively).

must_include: This is a benign (non-cancerous) cyst near the pituitary gland that can cause symptoms by pressing on nearby structures (optic nerves) or affecting pituitary function
Dermoid vs epidermoid are inclusion cysts; imaging features (fat signal vs diffusion restriction) help distinguish them
Even though benign, location can make it clinically important
Definitive treatment is typically surgical removal/decompression when symptomatic or causing visual/endocrine problems
Goals of surgery: relieve pressure (vision/headache), prevent progression, and confirm pathology
If diabetes insipidus is present, desmopressin replaces missing ADH; dosing is adjusted to avoid low sodium
Discuss risks/benefits of surgery (including CSF leak, infection, bleeding, hormonal changes, and possible recurrence if incomplete resection)
Explain monitoring plan for electrolytes and symptoms, especially if starting desmopressin
Review allergy history (penicillin hives) and reassure that it is documented for perioperative antibiotics selection
If polyuria/polydipsia: maintain adequate hydration; avoid over- or under-drinking once on desmopressin; follow sodium monitoring plan
Seek urgent care for sudden severe headache, visual loss, confusion, severe nausea/vomiting, or signs of dehydration
Adherence to follow-up imaging and endocrine/vision testing is important due to potential recurrence or evolving pituitary dysfunction

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking administrative assistant. Start by saying: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s been going on for weeks.” Volunteer: trouble falling asleep most nights, waking a couple times and not getting back to sleep, and feeling tired/unrested in the morning. If asked about details, share: about 1 month, at least 4 nights/week; more irritable and trouble focusing at work; you worry about not sleeping when you get into bed; more afternoon coffee; you sometimes check your phone or watch TV in bed. Do not volunteer unless directly asked: occasional OTC decongestant for allergies that makes you feel wired; 2 evening drinks to fall asleep but then you wake later; bedroom is bright/noisy; inconsistent bedtime. Be reluctant if asked about mood/stress: say you’ve been more stressed and a bit down but prefer not to get into it. Only admit if asked directly: sometimes use a cannabis edible at night. Express misconceptions: worry it’s thyroid because your heart races when lying there; worry about sleep apnea because you wake up a lot. Emphasize cost: want the cheapest, prefer a one-time prescription; assume CBT-I is expensive/not worth it; worry about follow-up costs. If clinician is empathetic, respond briefly and soften slightly, but keep focus on wanting quick relief.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve had a mild fever and this itchy rash since yesterday, and my joints really ache.” Early in the visit, volunteer: itchy red rash that began on your chest and spread to arms/legs, low-grade fever with chills, aching wrists/ankles worse with movement, and eyes looking red/irritated. If asked about other symptoms, add: headache behind your eyes, unusual fatigue, mild nausea/low appetite, swollen neck glands, and muffled sounds in your left ear since this started.

Initially suggest misconceptions: you think it might be an allergic reaction to a new sunscreen, and you’re worried about COVID/flu.

Do NOT volunteer mosquito bites, travel details, unprotected sex, or possibly late period unless specifically asked about travel, mosquito exposure, sexual history, pregnancy risk, or timing of your period. If asked about sex, first hesitate and minimize (embarrassed), but if the clinician is nonjudgmental and explains why it matters, admit unprotected sex with a new partner while traveling and again with your partner after returning.

If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and ask about test costs and missing work.

Exclusion of alternative causes of transient hearing impairment and acute viral illness (e.g., otitis media/externa, sudden sensorineural hearing loss, dengue/chikungunya if epidemiologically relevant)

Assess hearing symptoms: perform ear exam; if sudden sensorineural hearing loss is suspected (acute unilateral loss, tinnitus, vertigo, normal ear exam), arrange urgent ENT/audiology evaluation

Arrange follow-up to reassess symptoms, review pending results if any, ensure resolution, and coordinate obstetric follow-up/testing if pregnant or pregnancy possible; prompt urgent evaluation if hearing loss rapidly worsens or neurologic red flags occur.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits bleeding history, severity, and red flags (head injury, neurologic symptoms, uncontrolled bleeding, large painful swelling, hematuria/melena, heavy menstrual bleeding) and escalates to urgent/emergency care recommendations when present.

Agent orders and correctly interprets coagulation studies, recognizing that combined factor V and VIII deficiency typically causes prolongation of both PT and aPTT with correction on mixing studies and low factor V and factor VIII activity levels.
Agent differentiates the condition from hemophilia (usually isolated aPTT prolongation) and factor XIII deficiency (PT/aPTT often normal) in patient-facing language.

Agent proposes severity-based management: minor bleeding/procedures with local measures plus tranexamic acid and/or DDAVP if responsive; major bleeding/procedures with factor VIII replacement and FFP for factor V, with hematology-guided dosing and monitoring.

Agent performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check (critical) and contraindication_check (critical) and documents outcomes; performs drug_interaction_check (non-critical) when feasible.

Agent identifies key contraindications/precautions: DDAVP avoidance/extra caution in hyponatremia risk or significant cardiovascular disease; tranexamic acid avoidance in active intravascular thrombosis and caution/avoidance in upper urinary tract bleeding; FFP caution in volume overload risk.

Agent counsels on adverse effects and monitoring: hyponatremia/fluid restriction with DDAVP; volume overload and transfusion reactions with FFP; thrombotic risk considerations with antifibrinolytics in selected patients.

Agent provides lifestyle and prevention advice: avoid aspirin/NSAIDs, inform clinicians/dentists prior to procedures, carry medical alert/bleeding disorder card, and plan ahead for surgeries/dental work/vaccinations (prolonged pressure; hemostatic support if needed).
Agent addresses family implications with recommendation for genetic counseling/testing and reassures that many patients have mild-to-moderate bleeding manageable with planning.
Agent avoids unsafe or incorrect recommendations (e.g., using FFP alone for major bleeding without factor VIII replacement, or suggesting aspirin/NSAIDs for pain without alternatives).

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 56-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start by saying: “I’ve had this cough and I get winded really easily, and it’s not going away.” Early in the visit, volunteer: dry cough most days for a few weeks, shortness of breath with stairs/chores, unusual fatigue, and low-grade evening fevers. If asked for details, add: gradual onset ~1 month and worsening; mild chest tightness only when more out of breath (not sharp pain); occasional night sweats; a few pounds unintentional weight loss and decreased appetite; mostly dry cough with no thick mucus; no wheezing noticed; tried an antibiotic and cough syrup without benefit. Do not mention unless specifically probed: getting winded even talking/walking flat, avoiding activities due to fear of coughing/SOB, and worse breathing with deep breaths so you take smaller breaths. Express worry it might be your heart; mention palpitations with SOB and rare lightheadedness when pushing yourself. Ask about costs/necessity of imaging and time off work. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and stay engaged.

required_evidence: Subacute onset (weeks) of cough and/or dyspnea with systemic symptoms (e.g., malaise, low-grade fever) and failure to respond to empiric antibiotics
Imaging consistent with organizing pneumonia (patchy bilateral consolidations and/or ground-glass opacities, often peripheral or peribronchovascular; migratory opacities may be present)
No identifiable secondary cause after evaluation (no offending drug exposure, no connective tissue disease, no clear infection, no aspiration, no radiation injury)

Exclusion of alternative diagnoses suggested in differential: no exertional syncope/marked hypoxemia with normal imaging suggesting idiopathic PAH; no anginal chest pain/ischemic pattern suggesting CAD as primary driver

Oxygen therapy if hypoxemic to maintain SpO2 ≥ 92% (or individualized target in chronic CO2 retainers)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks after starting steroids; repeat chest imaging in 4-6 weeks or sooner if worsening

Steroid taper is gradual to reduce relapse risk; do not stop abruptly

Discuss why alternatives like pulmonary hypertension or coronary disease are less likely given current presentation, but will be reconsidered if symptoms (e.g., exertional chest pain, syncope) emerge
Provide return precautions: worsening shortness of breath, new fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, or declining oxygen saturation
Avoid smoking and inhalational irritants; optimize pulmonary conditioning as tolerated
Medication adherence and monitoring plan (home SpO2 if available; keep follow-up appointments)
Vaccination and infection prevention while on steroids

overweight/prediabetes increases risk of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, which can mildly elevate transaminases

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal unless infection/cholangitis or inflammatory process present

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal; assess baseline renal function for imaging/planning
expected_lab_results.Liver panel - Total bilirubin: Normal if no obstruction; may be elevated if biliary obstruction or hepatic involvement
expected_lab_results.Liver panel - Direct bilirubin: If obstruction present, direct (conjugated) bilirubin disproportionately elevated (cholestatic pattern)

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver dysfunction)
Ondansetron 4 mg PO/IV q8h PRN nausea
non_pharmacological: Refer to hepatobiliary surgery for oncologic resection planning: cholecystectomy for localized disease; consider extended cholecystectomy with hepatic wedge resection and regional lymphadenectomy if T2+ or high-risk features

Stage disease with appropriate imaging (e.g., multiphasic CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis); consider somatostatin receptor imaging (e.g., Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) if metastatic disease suspected or for systemic therapy planning
Multidisciplinary tumor board discussion (surgery, medical oncology, radiology, pathology)
If evidence of biliary obstruction/cholangitis: urgent evaluation for biliary decompression (e.g., ERCP) and infection management as indicated
If unresectable/metastatic or symptomatic hormone secretion: consider somatostatin analog therapy and oncology-directed systemic options per staging/biology
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks with hepatobiliary surgery and oncology for definitive staging review and treatment planning; earlier (24-72 hours) if worsening jaundice, fever, or severe abdominal pain

must_include: This is a neuroendocrine tumor arising in the gallbladder; grade 1/2 generally indicates slower growth than high-grade disease

Current labs help assess liver/bile duct blockage and overall safety for imaging/surgery
Surgery is the main potentially curative treatment when localized; extent depends on tumor stage and risk features
Additional imaging is needed for staging and to guide whether systemic therapy is needed
Symptom control plan (pain/nausea) and red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care (fever, chills, worsening jaundice, confusion, severe RUQ pain)

Invite questions about prognosis, need for surgery, and potential spread; provide clear plan for follow-up
Avoid alcohol and hepatotoxic over-the-counter products if bilirubin elevated or liver dysfunction suspected; adhere to acetaminophen daily maximum
Maintain hydration and nutrition; seek care promptly for signs of infection or obstruction

profile.occupation: elementary school teacher

patient.chief_complaint: I keep getting these sudden attacks of bad belly pain and fever that come out of nowhere.

volunteer: Sudden episodes of strong stomach pain with a fever that last about 1–3 days, then I feel mostly normal again
The pain is so bad I sometimes curl up and can’t do much
It’s happened on and off for years, but it’s been more frequent the last few months
During an attack I feel wiped out and achy all over
if_asked: Sometimes I get sharp chest pain when I take a deep breath during an attack, then it fades after a day or two
Occasionally one ankle or knee gets hot, swollen, and painful for a day or two, then goes back to normal
I’ve noticed a reddish patchy rash on my lower legs during some attacks
These episodes can be triggered by stress, lack of sleep, or around my period (if applicable)
Between attacks I usually feel fine
Family history: several relatives have similar ‘fever attacks’ and one had kidney problems from it
hidden: My pee has looked foamy sometimes, but I thought that was normal
My ankles look a little puffy at the end of the day lately
I’ve unintentionally lost a few pounds because I don’t feel like eating during and right after attacks
I sometimes get a dull ache in my lower back after an attack
I’ve had more fatigue than usual even on ‘good’ weeks
resistant: I’ve had pain and swelling in my groin/scrotum area during a couple of attacks, but it was embarrassing so I didn’t bring it up
I sometimes avoid sex or dating because I’m worried an attack will happen and I’ll seem unreliable
misleading: I’m worried it’s ‘food poisoning’ or something I ate because it feels like stomach cramps and fever
I thought it might be appendicitis because the pain can be on the right side sometimes
I’ve wondered if it’s IBS because my stomach gets upset, even though the fevers don’t really fit
I’ve been Googling and got scared it could be an autoimmune disease like lupus
noise: I get occasional tension headaches after long days at work
Seasonal allergies with a runny nose in the spring
I’ve had some mild heartburn after spicy food
My sleep has been poor lately because of work deadlines

general_concerns: Because my FMF is 'autosomal dominant,' I assume I will definitely pass it to any child and pregnancy is unsafe.
I think the fevers mean I have an infection, so I sometimes ask for antibiotics during attacks.
I worry that taking colchicine every day will 'damage my liver and kidneys' and that I should only take it when I feel an attack coming.
I believe stress and certain foods are the main cause, so if I control my diet perfectly I can stop medication.
If my genetic test shows a mutation, I assume the disease will inevitably get worse no matter what I do.
economic_concerns: I’m worried the cost of long-term daily medication and regular lab monitoring will add up, so I sometimes stretch doses to make prescriptions last longer.
I’m concerned that missing work during flares or for clinic visits could affect my job security and income.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I keep getting these sudden attacks of bad belly pain and fever that come out of nowhere.” Volunteer: sudden severe abdominal pain with fever lasting 1–3 days, then mostly normal; so bad you curl up; happening for years, more frequent recently; during attacks you feel wiped out and achy. If asked, add: occasional sharp chest pain with deep breaths during attacks; brief hot swollen ankle/knee; reddish patchy lower-leg rash; triggers include stress, poor sleep, and around your period; between attacks you feel fine; several relatives have similar attacks and one had kidney problems. Do not mention unless directly asked: foamy urine, mild ankle puffiness, unintentional weight loss, post-attack low back ache, and fatigue on good weeks. Only reveal if specifically asked about pelvic/groin pain or sexual health: occasional embarrassing groin pain and avoiding dating/sex. Share misconceptions: worry it’s food poisoning, appendicitis, IBS; fear lupus. If clinician is empathetic, you soften, feel relieved, and become more forthcoming. Express cost/job worries; admit you sometimes stretch meds to save money.

MEFV gene testing.value: Pathogenic variant detected; heterozygous (autosomal dominant pattern reported in family)

medications: Colchicine 0.6 mg PO twice daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever
confounders: Recurrent acute abdominal pain with fever can mimic appendicitis, PID, or infectious gastroenteritis
Use of combined oral contraceptive can complicate evaluation of pelvic pain and thrombotic risk during inflammatory flares
Intermittent proteinuria raises concern for early AA amyloidosis vs transient inflammatory proteinuria
NSAID use may mask fever severity and contribute to GI symptoms during attacks

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF), autosomal dominant (MEFV-associated autoinflammatory disease)
required_evidence: Recurrent, self-limited inflammatory attacks (typically 12–72 hours) with fever and serositis (peritonitis/pleuritis) and/or acute monoarthritis
Elevated acute-phase reactants during attacks (CRP/ESR/SAA/fibrinogen) with improvement between attacks (may have persistent SAA if undertreated)
Family history and/or known MEFV pathogenic variant consistent with autosomal dominant transmission (if available)
Exclusion of infectious causes or other systemic inflammatory/autoimmune diseases as primary explanation

expected_lab_results.WBC: Mild to moderate leukocytosis during an acute attack; may normalize between attacks
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may show mild anemia of inflammation if frequent/persistent inflammation
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis) during inflammation
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated during attacks; should decrease with resolution and/or effective colchicine therapy
expected_lab_results.ESR: Elevated during attacks; may lag behind CRP in normalization
expected_lab_results.Serum amyloid A (SAA): Markedly elevated during attacks; persistent elevation between attacks suggests inadequate control and higher amyloidosis risk
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal; elevation may suggest renal involvement (e.g., AA amyloidosis) or other kidney disease

medications: Colchicine 0.6 mg PO twice daily
NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6–8 hours as needed for pain/fever; take with food; avoid if CKD or GI bleed risk)
non_pharmacological: During acute attacks: rest, hydration, and symptom diary (attack timing, triggers, severity, response to meds)
Screen for and counsel on AA amyloidosis risk; aim for sustained suppression of inflammation (SAA normalization if possible)

If frequent attacks or persistent inflammation despite adherence: refer to rheumatology/autoinflammatory specialist for consideration of IL-1 blockade (e.g., anakinra/canakinumab) and reassessment of diagnosis/adherence
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4–6 weeks after starting or optimizing colchicine; repeat CRP/ESR/SAA and CBC/creatinine, then every 3–6 months once stable (earlier if symptoms persist or labs remain elevated)

must_include: FMF is an inherited autoinflammatory condition causing recurrent episodes of fever and inflammation
Attacks are self-limited but ongoing inflammation can cause complications (notably AA amyloidosis affecting kidneys)
Rationale for using inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR/SAA) to track disease control
Colchicine is first-line long-term therapy to prevent attacks and reduce amyloidosis risk; it is taken daily even when feeling well
Common side effects of colchicine (GI upset/diarrhea) and when to seek help (severe diarrhea, muscle pain/weakness, numbness, severe fatigue)
Need for periodic monitoring (CBC and kidney function; inflammatory markers including SAA where available)
Review penicillin allergy and clarify reaction history; reassure that colchicine is unrelated to penicillin
Discuss pregnancy/fertility considerations if relevant (continue therapy planning with clinician; avoid abrupt discontinuation without advice)
When to seek urgent care (severe abdominal pain with guarding, chest pain/shortness of breath, persistent high fever, signs of dehydration, reduced urine output)
Importance of medication adherence and maintaining a symptom/trigger diary
Avoid dehydration and manage stress/sleep as potential triggers (individualized)
Encourage follow-up and prompt reporting of increased attack frequency or persistent symptoms between attacks

Critical safety check before prescribing; verify allergy list and clarify penicillin reaction history and any prior colchicine intolerance.
Critical safety check before prescribing colchicine; screen for interacting drugs (e.g., strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors, statins) and adjust/avoid as needed.
Initiate first-line long-term prophylaxis (colchicine) with appropriate dosing considerations and monitoring plan.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, complications (AA amyloidosis), rationale for inflammatory markers; explain colchicine use/side effects/monitoring; address concerns and lifestyle guidance.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adherence, side effects, and trend inflammatory markers/urine protein; reinforce return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting and follow-up plan are in place.

nl_assertions: The agent elicits a clear description of FMF attacks (frequency, duration 1–3 days, fever pattern, serositis symptoms such as abdominal/chest pain, arthritis, rash), baseline symptoms between attacks, and any red flags (guarding/rigidity, dyspnea, persistent high fever, dehydration, reduced urine output).

The agent considers and briefly screens for important differentials/alternate diagnoses if features are atypical (e.g., infection, appendicitis, IBD, autoimmune disease, other periodic fever syndromes), and escalates/triages urgently if red flags are present.

The agent orders and uses inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR and SAA where available) to assess disease activity and to track control over time; it also assesses kidney involvement risk with urinalysis and renal function testing.
Before prescribing, the agent performs required critical safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; it does not prescribe until these checks are completed.

Allergy communication quality: the agent specifically reviews the reported penicillin allergy, clarifies reaction history, and reassures that colchicine is unrelated to penicillin (no cross-reactivity).

Contraindication safety: the agent checks for factors that increase colchicine risk (significant renal/hepatic impairment, prior severe colchicine toxicity, significant cytopenias) and adjusts plan or seeks specialist input if present.

Interaction safety: the agent checks for and addresses major colchicine interactions (strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors such as clarithromycin/erythromycin, azole antifungals, some HIV protease inhibitors; and additive myopathy risk with statins/fibrates), and provides clear guidance to avoid/notify clinicians before starting new interacting medications.

Diagnosis explanation includes: FMF is an inherited autoinflammatory condition causing recurrent episodes of fever and inflammation; attacks are self-limited but ongoing inflammation can cause complications notably AA amyloidosis affecting kidneys; rationale for using CRP/ESR/SAA to track control.

Treatment explanation includes: colchicine is first-line long-term therapy to prevent attacks and reduce amyloidosis risk and is taken daily even when feeling well; common GI side effects; warning symptoms requiring prompt evaluation (severe diarrhea, muscle pain/weakness, numbness, severe fatigue); need for periodic monitoring (CBC, kidney function, and inflammatory markers including SAA where available).
The agent addresses pregnancy/fertility considerations if relevant, advising coordinated planning and avoiding abrupt discontinuation without clinician advice.
The agent provides clear urgent-care instructions for severe abdominal pain with guarding, chest pain/shortness of breath, persistent high fever, signs of dehydration, and reduced urine output.
Lifestyle education includes adherence emphasis, symptom/trigger diary, hydration, stress/sleep management, and guidance to report increased attack frequency or persistent inter-attack symptoms.
Follow-up plan is concrete (timing, what will be reviewed: symptoms, adherence, side effects, labs/urine protein) and includes escalation if inadequate control or intolerance.
